text:
Your company is being graded on two report cards right now, and they don't grade the same work.

Your board grades you on Rule of 40, NRR, and capital efficiency. The valuation gap between hitting that bar and missing it is roughly 3x right now. Brutal math.

Your buyers grade you on something else. Security posture. Time-to-value under 45 days. A named reference in their vertical. Whether someone they trust mentioned you before they ever opened a tab.

The part most PMs miss: buyers fill most of their shortlist on day one, and most deals close with a vendor from that initial list. The "evaluation phase" you're optimizing for is mostly confirmation of a decision already made.

So a framework I've started using: every quarter, list your top 5 product investments and ask which scoreboard they move. Board scoreboard, buyer scoreboard, or both.

If everything moves only the board scoreboard, you'll post good numbers and lose deals you should have won. If everything moves only the buyer scoreboard, you'll win deals into a multiple that disappoints the people who decide your budget next year.

Where it breaks down: early-stage companies pre-PMF. You don't have two scoreboards yet. You have one, and it's survival.

For everyone past that point, the question worth asking your leadership team this month: which scoreboard did our last roadmap serve, and can you prove it?

#ProductManagement #B2BSaaS #ProductStrategy #SaaS #RevenueMetrics #KPIs #ProductMetrics
topic: Metrics that matter to buyers and boards
